Dear friends, welcome to the Factograph channel.  Today we will talk about mutant animals.  Have you ever wondered how far nature can go in its experiments, or what secrets lie behind the laboratory doors where scientists try to unravel the mysteries of genetics?  In this video, we'll dispel myths and discuss the reality behind the word mutant.  We'll look at the changes that occur in animals as a result of mutations, as well as the experiments being conducted in scientific laboratories around the world.  We will show you the most amazing, sometimes frightening, examples of when nature or man interferes with the natural course of events.  Get ready for an exciting journey into a world where science meets fantasy. Subscribe to our channel, click the bell and stay with us.   There is a lot of interesting things ahead.  Now let's go. If you look in a biology textbook, you will find the definition that a mutation is a permanent change in the genome, the complete set of genetic information contained in an organism, that has arisen suddenly or was induced artificially.  It is persistent because it can be inherited by the descendants of a given cell or organism.  The term mutation was first proposed to geneticists by Hugodefries in 1901.  Depending on the nature of the genome change, several types of mutations are distinguished: genomic, chromosomal and gene.  As a result of genomic mutations, the number of chromosomes changes.  For example, as a result of polyplaidy, the number of chromosome sets increases.  As a result of hapleidy, the number of chromosome sets decreases.  In aneoplaidy, several chromosomes are lost or added to the genome.  Chromosomal mutations include changes in the structure of chromosomes.  Deletions are the loss of a section of a chromosome.  Duplications are the doubling of a section of a chromosome.  Inversions are rotations of a section by 180°.  Translocations are the transfer of a section of a chromosome to a non-homologous chromosome. Fragmentation is the breakup of a chromosome into two or more parts.  For example, a dilation of a region of the fifth human chromosome leads to the development of a hereditary disease, Lizhen syndrome.  Gene mutations are changes in the nucleotide sequence of a specific gene.  For example, mutations in the BRCA, BRCA1, and BRCA2 genes increase the incidence of breast cancer .  Why do such anomalies occur?  According to the mutation theory, developed by Hugo De Vries more than a century ago, mutations occur spontaneously in nature and can be repeated.  They are common to all living beings. Mutations are one of the fundamental driving forces of evolution. They fuel population variability. And mutations beneficial for survival are fixed in new generations. Modern scientists claim that mutation is a change in the genes of a living being due to external influence on the fetus at the time of its formation.  At a certain point, mutagens develop in the body .  They are the ones who are responsible for genetic abnormalities.  Mutations in animals are an important mechanism leading to changes in genetic material, which influences the evolution of species and the diversity of wildlife.  All mutations in animals are divided into three categories: harmful mutations, neutral mutations, and beneficial mutations. Harmful mutations can cause serious diseases or abnormalities, such as cancer or genetic defects.  For example, animals may exhibit mutations that result in deformities or decreased fertility.  Beneficial mutations help to adapt to the environment. For example, resistance to certain diseases or changing color to protect against predators.  These mutations can increase the chances of survival and reproduction of individuals.  Neutral mutations are changes in the genetic code that have no apparent effect on the survival or reproduction of an organism. For example, animals exhibit many variations in coat color that do not affect their health or ability to survive.  However, not always what you hear about mutants is true.  The most striking example is the Chernobyl Exclusion Zone.  Let's not take into account the completely speculative blog posts and eyewitness accounts of two-headed cannibals spotted in the exclusion zone .  After all, mutants are usually understood to mean animals that received a dose of radiation after a nuclear power plant disaster.  We are talking about wild animals living in the vicinity of Pripyat: roe deer, wolves, bears, foxes, wild boars and domestic animals, such as dairy cows, left to graze freely after the evacuation of people .  In the nearly four decades since the disaster, photographs and videos of supposedly mutated animals, birds, and fish have repeatedly appeared online.  And surely each of you has seen one of the most famous recordings, which depicts giant catfish swimming in the ChS cooling pond .  Some of these catfish, according to eyewitnesses, reach several meters in length and weigh more than 100 kg.  Such observations, as well as photographs and videos of catfish swimming in the pond, at one time became quite popular on the Internet.  Yes, these radiation-soaked catfish are certainly impressive, but despite the overblown headlines, radiation is not the cause of their growth.  The point here is that the Chernobyl reactor coolant reservoir offers catfish populations an isolated habitat, free of predators and filled with abundant prey.  Catfish are active predators and scavengers that are known to feed on fish, amphibians, worms, birds, and small mammals. In fact, this fish will eat almost anything, alive or dead, that can fit into their very large mouth.  And here in Chernobyl, they have virtually no competition for food. Therefore, in the Chernobyl zone, despite the high level of radiation, no evidence was found of the existence of mutant animals in the generally accepted sense. Although some studies point to genetic changes in animals, scientists have not discovered creatures with monstrous anomalies, as often depicted in science fiction.  [music] After the accident, when people left the area, nature began to recover and the number of wild animals increased significantly.  especially large predators such as wolves and lynxes.  Also, previously unknown species are now being observed in the area .  Why are there no mutant animals in a place so heavily poisoned by radiation ?  Because the mutations that usually occur under the influence of radiation are so fatal that the animal simply cannot be born.  It dies in the womb. But if there are no mutant animals in the Chernobyl zone , this does not mean that they do not exist at all.  Lately, the world has been increasingly excited by news of the birth of unusual animals with extra or, conversely, missing body parts.  However, most mutants immediately pass away to the next world.  But there are also those among them who live a completely normal life and even become everyone's favorites.  These animals have unique characteristics that are the result of genetic mutations that usually occur randomly.  Let's take a look at the most famous mutants that many foreign publications wrote about at one time.  Two-headed snakes.  These creatures are incredibly rare.  However, it is snakes, among all other animals, that are most prone to such mutations.  Throughout human history, discoveries of two-headed snakes have left a significant mark on folklore.  What is the Hydra worth, which was defeated by Hercules and the familiar snake Gorynych.  It was probably they who formed the basis of the first myths about snakes with several heads.  Why can a snake have two heads?  There are two reasons for this .  The first is a failure in the development of the embryo.  Occurs in reptiles, birds, and humans.  This is how Siamese twins are born. Sometimes the division stops halfway, and the result is one body, two heads.  The second is a genetic mutation. Anything from background radiation to environmental chemistry could be to blame.  In countries with poor ecology, such snakes are found more often.  But this is not a rule, [the music] is just a coincidence that has not yet been refuted.  This is how two-headed snakes appear.  At first glance, they look like monsters.  In fact, they are tragicomic mutants, for whom life is hard and death is a pity.  It would seem that two heads are better than one, but in nature such snakes do not live long.  The brains of both heads function completely independently. Two brains on one body do not know about each other and decide that they command the tail alone.  Command conflicts occur every second.  The left head wants to crawl to the right, and the right one wants to climb a tree.  During feeding, everyone tries to grab a bigger piece for themselves, and if the neck length is enough, the animals can even fight among themselves.  A calf with two heads.  Several years ago, a calf with two heads was born on a farm in the state of Tripura in northeastern India. The animal was diagnosed with a rare disease: polycephaly.  Polycephalic, that is, multi-headed, are a type of Siamese twins.  Typically, with this deviation, the animal has one body and two or three heads.  It is noted that most often such cubs die a few days after birth.  But there were cases when the animal lived up to 2 months.  Yes, such a mutation is not the only [music] case. The animal died after battling melanoma, a serious type of skin cancer that develops in the cells that produce melanin, the pigment that gives skin its color. Rathweiler Rommel Svetiligo. When melanocytes, the cells that produce melanin, malfunction, the skin simply loses pigmentation over time, resulting in white spots. The predisposition to vitiligo is often hereditary. Hercules and Tiangou dogs are another muscle-bound breed in the animal world, also originating from China.  In 2015, scientists from Guanzhou [music] bred dogs with super-strong muscles using genetic engineering.  To pump up the animal, scientists resorted to a trick familiar to us. The gene responsible for the production of myostatin was disabled in dogs.  Instead of traditional breeding, they made changes to the genes of 35 dog embryos.  As a result, the animals' bodies stopped producing the protein that controls muscle growth.  This is how the Beagle, Hercules and Tiangou breeds appeared, which have double the amount of muscle mass.  This change not only made the animals more muscular, but also increased their endurance.  According to Chinese geneticists, super-muscular hounds can be used as hunting dogs, as well as in police and military applications. However, the purpose of the experiment was different. Create dogs with DNA mutations that lead to Parkinson's disease and muscular dystrophy.  Scientists believe that the research will help better understand the nature of human diseases and find new methods to combat them. Scientists do not plan to specifically breed modified racers .  Chimera of man and pig. This phrase alone is already creepy. Why did scientists even come up with the idea of ​​creating human- animal hybrids?  After all, such experiments with genes are still considered quite controversial from an ethical point of view.  However, there is another side.  The goal is to create entire farms with animals that can become suitable organ donors for patients in need. Surgeons have long since learned how to transplant human organs .  This requires donor material from a healthy person. Some organs, such as a single kidney, can be donated to a patient and the patient can survive.  But a donor heart can only be obtained from a deceased person.  Therefore, donor material from people is always in short supply, and it is impossible to significantly increase its supply.  There are also two alternatives.  Tissues from some animals can take root in the human body.  For example, a heart valve for humans can be made from pig biomaterial.  While it's possible to print living tissue layer by layer using a 3D printer capable of handling cells, we're still a long way from being able to print a working liver or lungs .  But what if animals could have human organs?  Impossible.  No, it's possible.  In 2017, an international team of geneticists in the United States succeeded in producing chimeric embryos containing a combination of human and pig cells.  To begin, biologists edited the embryo's genome and disabled two genes in order to prevent the development of its own kidneys.  Thus, a free space appeared in the body where foreign tissue could develop.  The study's authors then transformed human stem cells into embryonic-like cells and implanted them into early-stage pig embryos.  Before being implanted into mother pigs, the resulting embryos were grown for some time in a special nutrient medium suitable for both human and pig cells.  After 28 days, the pigs' pregnancies were terminated and the developed embryos were removed for analysis.  In five-chimeric organisms, the authors discovered kidneys with a normal structure.  They have formed tubules and cell rudiments.  Incredibly, these kidneys were 50-60% human cells.  This was the first important step towards growing human organs in a pig's body.  But scientists still needed to figure out how to make human cells transform into the necessary organs.  After all, the long-term goal of the research was to optimize technology for growing and transplanting human organs.  However, the experiment was terminated for ethical and legal reasons.  The thing is that if the Chimera animal were born, it would be, so to speak, a little humanoid.  It's scary to even imagine such a thing.  Glowing cats.   A team of South Korean scientists engaged in genetic engineering and cloning experiments have succeeded in breeding cats that glow in the dark. It sounds a bit wild, but [music] you must admit, it’s quite intriguing.  Back in 2007, they managed to clone three cats with the gene that produces the protein.  The protein fluoresces red and glows when exposed to ultraviolet light.  This gene served as a biomarker and was introduced into the DNA of animals in order to [music] illuminate tissues and observe the work of other cells.  This gene-modified cat has opened up new methods of treating hereditary diseases in the world of genetics.  This is very important.  Then American scientists began implementing the project based on the work of their Korean colleagues.  Through years of experiments, adding some DNA from glowing sea jellyfish to the genome of domestic cats, scientists have bred unusual kittens whose bodies contain green fluorescent protein.  The geneticists didn't stop there and crossed the glowing cats with each other.  The young generation of kittens was born with all the [music] traits of their transgenic parents.  The experiment demonstrated high efficiency of implantation of new genes.  In addition to the unusual luminescent effect, these cats are resistant to the AIDS virus. Scientists added the Macacresus gene, which protects against the immunodeficiency virus, to their DNA. Scientists believe that the research results will help both cats and humans, since our DNA is 90% identical.  In the near future, scientists plan to conduct a similar experiment on primates, and then on humans.  So don't be surprised if in 5-10 years you see the Little Green Man at night.  This is not the Hulk or an alien, but simply a mutant with jellyfish proteins, immune to the immunodeficiency virus.  [music] Overall, glowing cats aren't a bad idea either, as long as it doesn't harm their health.  Mutants are constantly being born in the animal kingdom , that's a fact.  Some of them are the result of natural processes, while others are the fruit of human intervention, aimed at great scientific goals or associated with man-made factors.  In conclusion of our journey through the world of animal mutants, I would like to say: the devil is not as black as he is painted.  On the one hand, science fiction often paints pictures of horrific creatures created by genetic experiments.  On the other hand, real research shows that mutations are a natural process that can lead to both positive and negative consequences. Laboratory experiments with animals open new horizons in understanding biology and genetics. Gene editing technologies can help fight diseases.  But we must also remember the ethical side and be careful that we do not go too far in our attempts to better understand human nature .  Remember that behind every scientific innovation there are living beings, and our attitude towards them should be based on good intentions.  After all, our knowledge and technology should serve for the good, not for the harm.  That's all from us, friends.  Thank you for watching.  Don't forget to share in the comments which mutant animal surprised you the most .

Welcome, friends, to the Factograph channel.  Today we'll talk about animals that have played a key role in shaping our history. While smart, affectionate, and friendly domestic rats are a favorite among pet lovers, wild rats that live in basements, sewers, dumps, and warehouses are repulsive and disgusting.  This is not surprising.  For thousands of years, rats have coexisted with humans.  As soon as people learned to grow crops and preserve them, rats appeared nearby. Moreover, everything that a person eats is also food for them.  And rats already consume a lot of things that humans don’t eat.  Clothes, books, candles, bones, tree bark, soap.  No mammal in the history of civilization has caused as much harm as rats, but that's just the tip of the iceberg.   A much greater danger is that rats are also very dangerous for humans.  They are carriers of at least thirty diseases dangerous to humans, including such terrible ones as plague, leprosy, and leprosy.  Rats, or more precisely their companions, fleas, were the culprits of the monstrous plague epidemic, which in the mid-14th century, according to various sources, wiped out between a third and half of Europe's population.   The plague bacillus was transmitted by fleas that lived on rats.  When the rat died, the fleas [music] jumped onto the person.  As a result, the plague, which was nicknamed the Black Death, changed the further course of European history.  Pigeons.  Today, pigeons have an unenviable reputation. Flatworms live in salt and fresh water bodies, less often on land in highly humid habitats. The most characteristic feature of flatworms is reflected in their name. Their body is mainly a layer of soft connective tissue - parenchyma, inside which there are channels that make up the digestive, excretory and reproductive systems of the worm.
